stem: A finite group \( G \) has order \( [[g]] \), and \( H \) is a subgroup with \( |H| = [[h]] \). By Lagrange's theorem, find the index \( [G:H] \), i.e. the number of distinct left cosets of \( H \) in \( G \).
distractor_rationales: This is \( |G| \) itself, forgetting to divide by \( |H| \).
This is \( |H| \), the wrong quantity from Lagrange's relation.
This subtracts \( |H| \) from \( |G| \) instead of dividing.
This adds \( |H| \) to \( |G| \) instead of dividing.
This is off by one above the correct index.
This is off by one below the correct index.

stem: Let \( G = \mathbb{Z}_{[[n]]} \) (integers mod \([[n]]\) under addition) and let \( H = \langle [[a]] \rangle = \{0, [[a]], 2\cdot[[a]], \dots\} \bmod [[n]] \) be the cyclic subgroup generated by \([[a]]\). Using Lagrange's theorem, find the index \( [G:H] \).
solution: The element \([[a]]\) has order \( |H| = [[n]]/\gcd([[n]],[[a]]) \) in \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[n]]} \). By Lagrange's theorem, \( [G:H] = |G|/|H| = [[n]]/([[n]]/\gcd([[n]],[[a]])) = \gcd([[n]],[[a]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is \( |H| \) itself, the wrong side of Lagrange's relation.
This is \( |G| \), forgetting to account for \( H \) at all.
This mistakes the generator \( [[a]] \) itself for the index.
This subtracts the index from \( n \) instead of computing \( \gcd \) correctly.
This is off by one above the correct index.
This is off by one below the correct index.

stem: Let \( G = S_{[[n]]} \) act on \( \{1,2,\dots,[[n]]\} \), and let \( H = \operatorname{Stab}(1) \) be the stabilizer of the point \(1\) (all permutations fixing \(1\)), so \( H \cong S_{[[n]]-1} \) and \( |H| = ([[n]]-1)! \). Using Lagrange's theorem, find the index \( [G:H] \).
solution: By Lagrange's theorem, \( [G:H] = |G|/|H| = [[n]]!/([[n]]-1)! = [[answer]] \), matching the size of the orbit of \(1\) under \(S_{[[n]]}\).
distractor_rationales: This is \( |G| = [[n]]! \) itself, forgetting to divide by \( |H| \).
This is \( |H| = ([[n]]-1)! \), the wrong side of Lagrange's relation.
This resembles the answer but subtracts 1 from \(n\) instead of computing the quotient.
This multiplies instead of using the factorial quotient.
This is off by one above the correct index.
This is off by one below the correct index.

stem: Let \( G = S_{[[n]]} \) act on \( \{1,\dots,[[n]]\} \), and fix a subset \( A \) of size \([[k]]\). Let \( H \) be the subgroup of permutations that map \(A\) to itself (setwise), so \( H \cong S_{[[k]]} \times S_{[[n]]-[[k]]} \) and \( |H| = [[k]]!\cdot([[n]]-[[k]])! \). Using Lagrange's theorem, find the index \( [G:H] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the number of ORDERED \(k\)-tuples, forgetting to divide by \(k!\).
This counts sequences with repetition, an unrelated formula.
This uses one fewer element in the ground set.
This uses one extra element in the ground set.
This is off by one above the correct index.
This is off by one below the correct index.

stem: Let \( G = D_{[[n]]} \) be the dihedral group of order \(2[[n]]\) (symmetries of a regular \([[n]]\)-gon), and let \( H \) be the subgroup of rotations by multiples of \([[d]]\) steps, so \( |H| = [[n]]/[[d]] = [[h]] \). Using Lagrange's theorem, find the index \( [G:H] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the rotation step \([[d]]\), not the index in the full dihedral group.
This is \( |G| \) itself, forgetting to divide by \( |H| \).
This is \( |H| \), the wrong side of Lagrange's relation.
This computes the index of \(H\) only inside the rotation subgroup, ignoring the reflections.
This is off by one above the correct index.
This is off by one below the correct index.
